content.text: Elasticity vs Scalability: The Distinction

Scalability and elasticity are related but distinct properties. Scalability is the architectural ability of a system to handle more load — a system is scalable if it can be made larger. Elasticity is the operational ability of a system to automatically grow and shrink in response to demand in real time, without human intervention.

A traditional database on a beefy server is not scalable at all — it has a hard ceiling. An on-premises server farm can be scalable (you can add more servers) but is not elastic (adding servers takes weeks and the process is manual). AWS infrastructure is both: the platform can grow to planetary scale, and resources are added or removed within seconds automatically.

attrs.alt: Fixed capacity model showing waste zones and outage risk vs elastic capacity tracking demand curve precisely
attrs.title: Elasticity vs Fixed Capacity — Waste and Risk Over Time

content.text: Property
Scalability
Elasticity
Definition
Can the system handle more load?
Does capacity automatically match current demand?
Direction
Usually only up (add capacity)
Both directions — up and down
Trigger
Can be manual
Always automatic in response to demand
Cost benefit
Prevents capacity ceiling outages
Eliminates idle capacity cost AND prevents outages
AWS example
Manually changing instance type from m5.large to m5.4xlarge
Auto Scaling Group adding/removing EC2 instances on CloudWatch alarms
Why Elasticity Matters for Cloud Economics

The economic argument for cloud is inseparable from elasticity. On-premises infrastructure must be provisioned for the worst-case peak load. Cloud infrastructure is provisioned for the current load. The gap between the two — idle capacity — is the primary cost difference between the two models.
Average server utilisation on-premises is 15–20 %:
The remainder is over-provisioned headroom. An elastic system targets 60–70 % utilisation, paying for roughly 3–4× less idle capacity.
Seasonal workloads cost the same all year on-premises:
A retail system provisioned for Black Friday load costs the same in February. Elastic cloud costs 90 % less in February when traffic is 10 % of peak.
Scale-in is as important as scale-out:

Many teams build Auto Scaling that adds instances but never removes them. This recovers the availability benefit of elasticity but not the cost benefit. Both directions must work for elasticity to deliver its economic promise.
Elasticity enables experimentation:
A new feature or service can be launched at zero base cost and scaled up only if it gains adoption. Failed experiments cost hours of cloud time rather than months of underused hardware.
Vertical Scaling (Scale Up)

Vertical scaling increases the resources of a single instance — more CPU, RAM, or disk. On AWS, this means changing the EC2 instance type or the RDS instance class. It requires a stop/start for most instance types and results in a brief outage window.
When vertical scaling is appropriate:
Stateful applications that cannot be distributed (single-writer databases, legacy monoliths with shared memory), or when the application is not yet worth re-architecting for horizontal scale.
Ceiling problem:
Vertical scaling has a hard limit — AWS offers EC2 instances up to 448 vCPUs and 24 TB RAM (u-24tb1 family). Beyond that, the architecture must change.
Not elastic by default:
Manual vertical scaling is not elastic. Only horizontal scaling combined with Auto Scaling provides true demand-tracking elasticity.
RDS instance class change:

RDS supports scheduled modification windows for instance class changes. The database is unavailable for a few minutes. For Multi-AZ, AWS fails over to the standby first, then upgrades the primary, limiting downtime to the failover window.

# Vertical scale an EC2 instance (requires stop/start)
# Step 1: Stop the instance
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123def456
aws ec2 wait instance-stopped --instance-ids i-0abc123def456

# Step 2: Change instance type
aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
  --instance-id i-0abc123def456 \
  --instance-type '{"Value": "m5.2xlarge"}'

# Step 3: Restart
aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123def456

# Vertical scale an RDS instance (scheduled for next maintenance window)
aws rds modify-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-postgres \
  --db-instance-class db.r6g.2xlarge \
  --apply-immediately false
Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out)

Horizontal scaling adds more instances of the same size behind a load balancer. This is the basis of all elastic architectures — it enables automatic scale-in/scale-out without any service interruption and has no practical upper limit on capacity.
Requires statelessness:
Each instance must be able to handle any request independently. Session state must live in a shared store (ElastiCache, DynamoDB) rather than in instance memory.
No ceiling:
An Auto Scaling Group can scale to thousands of instances. Amazon itself scales S3, EC2, and Lambda horizontally to absorb global demand.
Fault tolerance is a free benefit:
Running N instances across multiple AZs means the failure of one instance or one AZ only removes a fraction of capacity — traffic shifts to surviving instances automatically via the load balancer.
Works with Auto Scaling for elasticity:
The combination of a load balancer (distributes traffic), an Auto Scaling Group (adjusts fleet size), and CloudWatch alarms (trigger scaling events) constitutes a fully elastic horizontal tier.
EC2 Auto Scaling Groups
Core Configuration

An Auto Scaling Group (ASG) manages a fleet of EC2 instances as a unit. You define a minimum, desired, and maximum capacity. The ASG launches and terminates instances to maintain the desired count, replacing unhealthy instances automatically and adjusting desired count in response to scaling policies.

# Create an Auto Scaling Group across 3 AZs
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-tier-asg \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateName=web-server-lt,Version='$Latest' \
  --min-size 2 \
  --max-size 20 \
  --desired-capacity 2 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "subnet-aaa111,subnet-bbb222,subnet-ccc333" \
  --target-group-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/web-tg/abc123 \
  --health-check-type ELB \
  --health-check-grace-period 60

# Attach a Target Tracking policy (scale out when CPU > 60%)
aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-tier-asg \
  --policy-name CPUTargetTracking \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-configuration '{
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
      "PredefinedMetricType": "ASGAverageCPUUtilization"
    },
    "TargetValue": 60.0,
    "ScaleInCooldown": 300,
    "ScaleOutCooldown": 60
  }'
Scaling Policy Types
AWS supports four scaling policy types, each suited to different demand patterns and response-time requirements.
Policy Type
How it works
Best for
Target Tracking
Maintain a target metric value (e.g. CPU = 60 %). AWS automatically adds/removes instances to hold the target.
Most web tiers; simplest to configure and most reliable
Step Scaling
Add/remove different quantities of instances based on the magnitude of the breach (e.g. +2 at 60 % CPU, +5 at 80 % CPU).
Variable-response workloads where the scale-out quantity should match the severity of the load increase
Scheduled Scaling
Pre-scale at a known time (cron expression). Capacity is in place before load arrives.
Known events: business hours start, weekly batch jobs, marketing campaign launches
Predictive Scaling
ML model forecasts load 48 hours ahead based on historical patterns and pre-scales proactively.
Regular cyclic patterns (daily/weekly) where reactive scaling adds latency during ramp-up
Elastic Load Balancing

Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) is the distribution layer that makes horizontal scaling useful to callers. Without a load balancer, adding instances does not help because clients do not know which instance to call. ELB provides a single stable DNS endpoint that distributes traffic across the current healthy pool of instances — automatically including newly launched instances and excluding terminated or unhealthy ones.
Load Balancer Type
Layer
Best for
Application Load Balancer (ALB)
L7 HTTP/HTTPS
Web apps, microservices, path/host-based routing, WebSocket
Network Load Balancer (NLB)
L4 TCP/UDP
Very high throughput, static IPs, TLS passthrough, gaming, IoT
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
L3 IP
Inline virtual network appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS)

ELB itself is elastic — it automatically scales its own capacity to handle traffic increases without configuration. The per-LCU pricing (Load Balancer Capacity Unit) means you only pay for the throughput you actually use, not a fixed amount.
AWS Lambda: Inherent Elasticity

Lambda is the most elastic compute service on AWS. It scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions within seconds, and back to zero when demand drops — with no Auto Scaling configuration required. The unit of scaling is the individual function invocation, not an instance.
Concurrency model:
Each simultaneous invocation gets its own isolated execution environment. 1,000 concurrent requests = 1,000 parallel Lambda executions. AWS handles the provisioning transparently.
Account-level concurrency limit:

The default limit is 1,000 concurrent executions per account per region. This can be raised via Support ticket. Functions can also have reserved concurrency to prevent one function from consuming the account limit.
Cold starts:

A new execution environment takes 100–500 ms to initialise (depends on runtime and package size). Subsequent invocations reuse the warm environment with <1 ms overhead. Provisioned Concurrency pre-warms environments to eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive workloads.
Zero cost at zero demand:
Lambda costs nothing when it is not invoked. For workloads with intermittent or unpredictable demand, this is the most economically efficient compute model available.

# View current Lambda concurrency utilisation
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/Lambda \
  --metric-name ConcurrentExecutions \
  --dimensions Name=FunctionName,Value=my-api-handler \
  --start-time $(date -d '1 hour ago' --iso-8601=seconds) \
  --end-time $(date --iso-8601=seconds) \
  --period 60 \
  --statistics Maximum \
  --query 'sort_by(Datapoints, &Timestamp)[*].{Time:Timestamp,Max:Maximum}' \
  --output table

# Set reserved concurrency to prevent a function from consuming the account limit
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name my-api-handler \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 500
Container Elasticity: ECS and EKS

Container orchestration platforms on AWS provide elasticity at two layers simultaneously: the application layer (adding/removing task or pod replicas) and the infrastructure layer (adding/removing EC2 nodes or Fargate capacity).
ECS Service Auto Scaling:
Scales the desired task count for an ECS service using Application Auto Scaling. Supports Target Tracking (keep ALB requests-per-target at a defined level) and Step Scaling.
EKS Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA):
Kubernetes-native mechanism that scales pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Combined with the Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter, it also adds/removes EC2 nodes as pod demand changes.
Karpenter for node provisioning:

AWS-built node provisioner for Kubernetes. Provisions the specific instance type that fits pending pods within seconds, using Spot when available. Far faster than the Cluster Autoscaler's ASG-based approach.
Fargate removes node management entirely:
With Fargate, there are no EC2 nodes to scale. Each task or pod gets exactly the vCPU and memory you specify. The platform handles all node-level provisioning transparently.

# Register ECS service with Application Auto Scaling
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/my-web-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --min-capacity 2 \
  --max-capacity 50

# Add a Target Tracking policy targeting ALB requests per target
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/my-web-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --policy-name alb-request-target \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration '{
    "TargetValue": 1000.0,
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
      "PredefinedMetricType": "ALBRequestCountPerTarget",
      "ResourceLabel": "app/my-alb/abc123/targetgroup/my-tg/def456"
    },
    "ScaleInCooldown": 300,
    "ScaleOutCooldown": 30
  }'
DynamoDB: Elastic Database Throughput

Traditional databases require vertical scaling (bigger instance) or complex sharding for more capacity. DynamoDB makes throughput elastic by design — capacity can grow or shrink without schema changes, migrations, or downtime.
Mode
How capacity is set
Best for
On-Demand
DynamoDB scales instantly to any traffic level. You pay per request.
Unpredictable traffic, new tables, event-driven workloads
Provisioned
You specify RCU/WCU. DynamoDB Auto Scaling adjusts within min/max bounds.
Predictable steady-state traffic where Reserved Capacity discounts apply
DynamoDB Auto Scaling
Application Auto Scaling adjusts provisioned RCU/WCU based on a target utilisation %
Predictable but variable load with cost savings over On-Demand at scale

# Enable DynamoDB Auto Scaling for write capacity
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \
  --service-namespace dynamodb \
  --resource-id table/orders \
  --scalable-dimension dynamodb:table:WriteCapacityUnits \
  --min-capacity 5 \
  --max-capacity 1000

aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --service-namespace dynamodb \
  --resource-id table/orders \
  --scalable-dimension dynamodb:table:WriteCapacityUnits \
  --policy-name DynamoDBWriteScaling \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration '{
    "TargetValue": 70.0,
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
      "PredefinedMetricType": "DynamoDBWriteCapacityUtilization"
    }
  }'
SQS: Decoupling for Demand Absorption

Amazon SQS is not a scaling mechanism itself, but it is the most important architectural enabler of elastic systems. By placing a queue between a producer and a consumer, the two can scale independently and the queue absorbs demand spikes without dropping messages.
Queue depth drives scale-out:
An Auto Scaling Group can scale based on the SQS
ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible
metric. As the queue grows, more consumers are added. As it drains, consumers are terminated.
Lambda + SQS event source mapping:

Lambda can consume SQS as a trigger. AWS automatically adjusts the number of parallel Lambda executions based on queue depth, up to the concurrency limit. You pay only for the Lambda invocations triggered.
Fan-out pattern with SNS + SQS:
A single SNS message can fan out to multiple SQS queues, each with its own consumer fleet scaling independently. This decouples producers from the full set of downstream consumers.
Protects downstream systems:

If a database or external API has a fixed throughput limit, a queue prevents burst traffic from overwhelming it. Consumers process at the maximum sustainable rate while the queue buffers excess demand.

# Scale EC2 workers based on SQS queue depth
# Add a CloudWatch alarm that triggers when queue > 1000 messages
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name sqs-queue-depth-high \
  --metric-name ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible \
  --namespace AWS/SQS \
  --dimensions Name=QueueName,Value=order-processing-queue \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 60 \
  --threshold 1000 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:autoscaling:us-east-1:123456789012:scalingPolicy:abc:policy-name/scale-out

# Configure Lambda to consume SQS (event source mapping)
aws lambda create-event-source-mapping \
  --function-name order-processor \
  --event-source-arn arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:order-processing-queue \
  --batch-size 10 \
  --scaling-config '{"MaximumConcurrency": 100}'
Stateless Architecture: The Prerequisite for Elasticity

An application is elastic only if individual instances are interchangeable — any instance can handle any request. This requires application state to be externalised to shared storage rather than held in instance memory.
HTTP session state → ElastiCache or DynamoDB:

Store session tokens in Redis (ElastiCache) with a TTL. Any instance can look up the session without sticky routing. If an instance is terminated, sessions survive because they are in the external store, not in instance memory.
User-uploaded files → S3:
Files written to an EC2 instance's local disk are lost when that instance terminates. Write all uploads directly to S3 (via pre-signed URL or the SDK) so any instance in the fleet can access them.
Database connections → connection pooling:
A rapidly scaling fleet can exhaust database connection limits. Use RDS Proxy to pool and multiplex connections so hundreds of instances share a bounded connection pool.
Configuration → Parameter Store or environment variables:

Never bake environment-specific configuration into the AMI or container image. Retrieve it at startup from Systems Manager Parameter Store or Secrets Manager so new instances always get current configuration.

import boto3
import json

# Store session data in ElastiCache (Redis) — stateless instance pattern
import redis

r = redis.Redis(
    host='my-redis-cluster.abc123.ng.0001.use1.cache.amazonaws.com',
    port=6379,
    ssl=True
)

def get_session(session_id: str) -> dict:
    data = r.get(f'session:{session_id}')
    return json.loads(data) if data else {}

def save_session(session_id: str, data: dict, ttl_seconds: int = 3600) -> None:
    r.setex(f'session:{session_id}', ttl_seconds, json.dumps(data))

# Retrieve configuration from SSM Parameter Store at startup
ssm = boto3.client('ssm')
config = ssm.get_parameter(
    Name='/myapp/prod/db_connection_string',
    WithDecryption=True
)['Parameter']['Value']
Caching to Reduce Scaling Requirements
Caching reduces the load on the components that are hardest to scale (databases, external APIs), allowing the overall system to serve more traffic without proportional scaling of those bottlenecks.
Amazon ElastiCache (Redis/Memcached):
In-memory cache that serves reads at sub-millisecond latency. A cache hit rate of 80 % means your database only processes 20 % of requests, allowing it to stay on a smaller instance.
CloudFront for static and dynamic content:

Caches responses at AWS edge locations. A cache hit at the CDN never reaches your origin server. Cache-Control headers control TTL — even a 5-second TTL for a popular API endpoint can absorb traffic spikes.
DynamoDB DAX (read cache):

An in-memory cache layer for DynamoDB that serves reads from memory at microsecond latency. Reduces read RCU consumption on the DynamoDB table, which is particularly valuable for heavily read hot-key patterns.
Service Quotas and Scaling Limits

Elastic architectures can scale faster than AWS service limits allow if those limits are not proactively increased. Every AWS service has default quotas that constrain scaling. Understanding and proactively increasing limits prevents limit-induced outages during growth events.
EC2 vCPU limits per instance family:
Auto Scaling Groups are capped by the account's regional vCPU quota per instance family. A scale-out event that hits the quota will fail to launch new instances silently.
Lambda concurrent execution limit:
Default 1,000 concurrent executions per region. A viral API spike that drives 5,000 concurrent requests will be throttled at 1,000. Request limit increases before launch events.
AWS Service Quotas console:
Lists all quotas for all services. Many limits can be increased with a single-click request directly from the console. For critical services, request increases before you need them.

# View current service quotas for EC2
aws service-quotas list-service-quotas \
  --service-code ec2 \
  --query 'Quotas[?contains(QuotaName, `vCPU`)].{Name:QuotaName,Value:Value,Unit:Unit}' \
  --output table

# Request a quota increase programmatically
aws service-quotas request-service-quota-increase \
  --service-code lambda \
  --quota-code L-B99A9384 \
  --desired-value 5000

# View the status of your quota requests
aws service-quotas list-requested-service-quota-changes-by-service \
  --service-code lambda \
  --query 'RequestedQuotas[*].{Name:QuotaName,Status:Status,Requested:DesiredValue}' \
  --output table
The Cost Model of Elasticity

Elasticity changes the shape of the cost curve from flat-and-fixed to demand-correlated. Understanding this model helps set expectations for variable monthly bills and design appropriate budget alerts.
Scenario
Fixed provisioning cost
Elastic cloud cost
Normal weekday (50 % of peak)
$3,000 (full fleet always on)
$1,500 (half the instances)
Peak hour (100 % of demand)
$3,000 (planned for this)
$3,000 (full fleet during peak)
Off-peak night (5 % of demand)
$3,000 (same cost all night)
$150 (minimum 2-instance floor)
Monthly total (example)
~$35,000 (61 % reduction)

The minimum floor (Auto Scaling min-size × instance cost) is the baseline on-demand cost. Commitment discounts (Savings Plans, Reserved Instances) applied to this floor reduce the baseline further — combining elasticity with commitment pricing achieves the best total outcome.
Design guideline:

Set the Auto Scaling minimum to the lowest level that meets your availability SLA (typically 2–3 instances across 3 AZs), not to the average expected traffic. Auto Scaling handles ramp-up; the minimum handles constant baseline costs.
Benefits of Elasticity and Scalability
Eliminates idle capacity cost — the fleet shrinks during off-peak periods and costs proportionally less, aligning infrastructure spend to actual business activity.
Removes capacity ceiling outages — when demand exceeds a fixed fleet, the result is errors or degradation. Elastic systems add capacity before the ceiling is reached.
Enables horizontal fault tolerance — multiple instances across AZs means no single instance failure causes an outage. Elasticity and high availability are complementary.
Removes the capacity planning bottleneck — teams ship products without waiting for server procurement cycles. Any experiment can run at minimal scale within minutes.
Common Pitfalls
Aggressive scale-in with insufficient cooldown:
A 60-second cooldown after scale-in can cause thrashing — remove, scale out, remove, scale out — if traffic is volatile. Set scale-in cooldown (300–600 seconds) conservatively.
Scaling without stateless design:
Adding instances to a stateful application without externalising session state causes request failures when the load balancer routes users to a different instance. Statelessness is not optional.
Not accounting for scale-out latency:

EC2 instances take 60–180 seconds from scale trigger to handling traffic. A sudden spike that needs 10 instances in 30 seconds cannot be met reactively. Use Predictive Scaling or Scheduled Scaling for known peaks.
Scaling the wrong layer:
If the bottleneck is the database (not the app tier), scaling web servers solves nothing. Profile where the constraint is before adding Auto Scaling.
Key Takeaways
Scalability is the ability to handle more load; elasticity adds automatic bidirectional adjustment — elastic systems scale both up and down without human intervention.
EC2 Auto Scaling Groups with Target Tracking policies are the primary horizontal elasticity mechanism for EC2 — they maintain a target metric rather than reacting to threshold breaches.
Lambda and DynamoDB On-Demand provide inherent elasticity with no Auto Scaling configuration — they scale to any demand level automatically and cost zero at zero demand.
Statelessness is the architectural prerequisite for elasticity — externalise all state (sessions, files, configuration) to shared stores so any instance can handle any request.
SQS queues decouple producers from consumers and allow independent scaling — queue depth is a more reliable scaling signal than CPU when processing time varies.
